writing_type: NIH R01 grant proposals (Significance and Specific Aims sections)
user_goals_summary: This user values grant writing that tells a compelling scientific story over information-dense reporting. They prioritize narrative urgency ('why now'), each aim having standalone scientific value while pointing forward, and ensuring that every claim feels earned rather than asserted. Cross-disciplinary accessibility and emotional coherence within paragraphs — especially strong, resolved closings — are central to how they judge quality.

rubric.name: Narrative Arc & 'Why Now' Urgency
rubric.description: The writing builds a story about why this work matters right now — not a list of facts or statistics. Each section should create tension around a gap, establish what has changed to make the question answerable, and make the reader feel urgency rather than just understand it.

dimensions.label: The scientific gap is framed as a tension or paradox the reader feels, not just a missing item in the literature
A clear 'why now' argument identifies what has changed (technically, conceptually, or methodologically) to make this work timely
The section reads as a narrative with logical progression, not as a sequence of disconnected claims or statistics
The reader feels the cost of not doing this work — urgency is conveyed through stakes, not just importance claims

rubric.name: Standalone-Yet-Connected Aim Architecture
rubric.description: Each aim produces something independently valuable — a deliverable, a resolved question, or a contribution the field gains regardless of other aims. At the same time, the aim naturally points forward so the reader senses a next chapter without feeling the aim is merely setup.

dimensions.label: The aim has at least one concrete deliverable or scientific contribution that exists even if subsequent aims fail
Language avoids framing the aim as 'establishing the foundation' or being valuable only because it enables later work
The aim's closing naturally raises the next scientific question as a consequence of its findings, not as a structural dependency
The aim reads as a complete scientific argument with its own hypothesis, logic, and resolution — not a methods summary

rubric.name: Earned Authority — Claims Grounded, Not Asserted
rubric.description: Every novelty claim, priority claim, or capability assertion is substantiated with specific technical, methodological, or empirical detail. A skeptical reviewer should finish each claim thinking 'I see why' rather than being asked to take it on faith.

dimensions.label: Novelty or 'first' claims are immediately followed by the specific detail that makes them true
Technical capabilities are described in concrete terms (mechanisms, specifications, design features) rather than generic competence language
Key methodological risks or challenges are named and reframed as the rationale for the work rather than hidden or hedged
Superlatives and strong claims are absent unless backed by evidence in the same passage

rubric.name: Cross-Disciplinary Accessibility
rubric.description: Technical arguments include plain-language moments that let non-specialist reviewers feel the significance without the prose being dumbed down. The writing is fluent in domain-specific terms but periodically surfaces to a register any scientifically literate reader can follow.

dimensions.label: At least one sentence per major paragraph translates the technical point into language a non-specialist reviewer can immediately grasp
Technical precision is maintained — accessibility is achieved through additional clarity, not by removing specificity
Key arguments are framed to resonate across the likely reviewer disciplines, not just the primary field
Specialized terms are introduced in context so their meaning is inferrable even to adjacent-field reviewers

rubric.name: Paragraph-Level Emotional Coherence
rubric.description: Each paragraph maintains a consistent emotional register from beginning to end. The closing carries the same weight as the paragraph's strongest moment, and the reader feels 'inside' the argument rather than observing it from outside.

dimensions.label: The emotional intensity does not abruptly shift within a paragraph (e.g., from compelling to bureaucratic)
The paragraph's closing sentence carries emotional and rhetorical weight equal to or greater than its strongest internal moment
There is at least one moment per paragraph where the pace slows enough for the reader to feel the significance of what's been said
The prose creates the sensation of following a mind thinking through a problem, not reading a pre-assembled argument

rubric.name: Strategic Framing of Preliminary Work & Personnel
rubric.description: Pilot data is positioned as a feasibility gate that reveals the next necessary question — not as a highlight reel of accomplishments. Personnel are framed in terms of scientific enablement (why this question is tractable because of them) rather than credential listing.

dimensions.label: Preliminary data is framed as proving feasibility and revealing what remains to be answered, not as previewing results
The pilot framing makes clear what the funded work will do that the pilot cannot, avoiding the 'already done' problem
Key personnel are introduced through the scientific or methodological condition they satisfy, not through qualifications alone
Personnel descriptions are woven into the scientific argument rather than appearing as credential inserts

rubric.name: Declarative Confidence & Rhetorical Resolution
rubric.description: Sentences assert and resolve rather than hedge or leave the reader suspended. Uncertainty is owned as rationale ('this is why the work matters') rather than presented as caveat. Paragraphs and aims close on declarative beats, not open questions.

dimensions.label: Paragraph and aim endings close on declarative statements rather than open questions or hedged possibilities
Scientific uncertainty is reframed as the justification for the work rather than acknowledged as a limitation
The proposal's team is the agent of action — sentences convey that the team is driving the work, not that conditions are passively favorable
The reader feels resolved at the end of each section, not suspended or left waiting for the next piece to make sense of what they just read

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: each aim must feel independently valuable — 'foundation' framing is a dealbreaker. This user iterates most heavily on paragraph closings; the ending of any section is where they judge whether the writing works. They respond well to direct pushback and structural diagnosis ('the problem isn't the sentence, it's where it's placed'), and they distinguish sharply between telling a reviewer something and making the reviewer feel it — 'earned not asserted' is a core value that applies to both technical claims and emotional register.

rubric.name: Paragraph Closing Resolution
rubric.description: Every major paragraph or section closes with a declarative beat that resolves the argument rather than leaving the reader suspended. Closings should assert what the work is positioned to do, not merely name what remains unknown — the difference between 'here is what we will find out' and 'here is what we don't yet know.'

dimensions.label: The closing sentence makes a statement or claim rather than posing an open question or naming a gap without resolution
The closing positions the research team or proposal as the agent of resolution, not the problem as an unresolved mystery
The closing creates a sense of arrival — the reader feels the section is complete, not suspended mid-argument
The closing avoids rhetorical questions, trailing clauses, or forward gestures that leave resolution to a later section

rubric.name: Standalone-Yet-Connected Aim Architecture
rubric.description: Applies to Specific Aims sections, not Significance sections. Each aim produces something independently valuable — a deliverable, a resolved question, or a contribution the field gains regardless of other aims. At the same time, the aim naturally points forward so the reader senses a next chapter without feeling the aim is merely setup.

dimensions.label: The aim has at least one concrete deliverable or scientific contribution that exists even if subsequent aims fail
Language avoids framing the aim as 'establishing the foundation' or being valuable only because it enables later work
The aim's closing naturally raises the next scientific question as a consequence of its findings, not as a structural dependency
The aim reads as a complete scientific argument with its own hypothesis, logic, and resolution — not a methods summary

rubric.name: Strategic Framing of Preliminary Work & Personnel
rubric.description: Pilot data is positioned as a feasibility gate that reveals the next necessary question — not as a highlight reel of accomplishments. Personnel are framed in terms of scientific enablement (why this question is tractable because of them) rather than credential listing.

dimensions.label: Preliminary data is framed as proving feasibility and revealing what remains to be answered, not as previewing results
The pilot framing makes clear what the funded work will do that the pilot cannot, avoiding the 'already done' problem
Claims that tools, methods, or infrastructure are 'ready' are accompanied by the specific gap that still requires funded investigation — readiness alone does not close the scientific question
Key personnel are introduced through the scientific or methodological condition they satisfy, not through qualifications alone
Personnel descriptions are woven into the scientific argument rather than appearing as credential inserts

writing_type: NIH grant proposals (significance sections, specific aims, and related narrative components)
user_goals_summary: The user writes NIH proposals that read as compelling scientific narratives rather than literature reviews. They prioritize urgency, earned authority over assertion, and prose that lets non-specialist reviewers feel the stakes without being dumbed down. They are highly attuned to structural vulnerabilities reviewers will notice — aims that sound contingent, claims that sound ungrounded, or framing that accidentally undermines the case for funding.

rubric.name: Narrative Arc & 'Why Now' Urgency
rubric.description: The writing builds a story about why this work matters right now — not a list of facts or statistics. Each section should create tension around a gap, establish what has changed to make the question answerable, and make the reader feel urgency rather than just understand it.

dimensions.label: The scientific gap is framed as a tension or paradox the reader feels, not just a missing item in the literature

The section reads as a narrative with logical progression, not as a sequence of disconnected claims or statistics
The reader feels the cost of not doing this work — urgency is conveyed through stakes, not just importance claims

rubric.name: Earned Authority — Claims Grounded, Not Asserted
rubric.description: Every novelty claim, priority claim, or capability assertion is substantiated with specific technical, methodological, or empirical detail. A skeptical reviewer should finish each claim thinking 'I see why' rather than being asked to take it on faith. Descriptions of existing tools or data must not accidentally make the scientific challenge seem trivial or already solved.

dimensions.label: Novelty or 'first' claims are immediately followed by the specific detail that makes them true
Technical capabilities are described in concrete terms (mechanisms, specifications, design features) rather than generic competence language
Key methodological risks or challenges are named and reframed as the rationale for the work rather than hidden or hedged

Descriptions of available tools, data, or existing work do not accidentally collapse the perceived difficulty of the scientific challenge — the gap should feel harder to close as context accumulates, not easier
Superlatives and strong claims are absent unless backed by evidence in the same passage

The closing creates a sense of arrival — the reader feels the section is complete, not suspended mid-argument
The closing avoids rhetorical questions, trailing clauses, or forward gestures that leave resolution to a later section

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: the writing must read as narrative, never as literature review — this user will reject competent prose that lacks story. They have a sharp instinct for structural vulnerabilities reviewers will exploit: aims that sound contingent, pilot data that sounds too complete, or framing that accidentally undermines the case for funding. When offering options, lean toward scientifically honest framing over rhetorically impressive framing — this user consistently chooses the version that feels more rigorous rather than more polished.

rubric.description: Technical arguments include plain-language moments that let non-specialist reviewers feel the significance without the prose being dumbed down. dimensions.label: At least one sentence per major paragraph translates the technical point into language a non-specialist reviewer can immediately grasp
Technical precision is maintained — accessibility is achieved through additional clarity, not by removing specificity

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is narrative momentum — they will always prioritize story over completeness, and any passage that feels like a 'literature review' or 'methods section' dropped into the significance will be flagged immediately. They are exceptionally sensitive to how paragraphs end: closings must be declarative, must carry emotional weight proportional to the paragraph's strongest moment, and must assert forward motion rather than pose open questions. When given options, they consistently choose urgency-through-temporal-cost framing ('every year this goes unanswered') over paradox or team-positioning framings. They respond well to being pushed back on when an instinct (e.g., adding patient-facing language in a technical paragraph) would undermine structural coherence — but the pushback must name the trade-off precisely.

rubric.name: Strategic Framing of Preliminary Work & Personnel
rubric.description: Pilot data is positioned as a feasibility gate that reveals the next necessary question — not as a highlight reel of accomplishments. Personnel are framed in terms of scientific enablement (why this question is tractable because of them) rather than credential listing.

dimensions.label: Preliminary data is framed as proving feasibility and revealing what remains to be answered, not as previewing results
The pilot framing makes clear what the funded work will do that the pilot cannot, avoiding the 'already done' problem
Claims that tools, methods, or infrastructure are 'ready' are accompanied by the specific gap that still requires funded investigation
Key personnel are introduced through the scientific or methodological condition they satisfy, not through qualifications alone
Personnel descriptions are woven into the scientific argument rather than appearing as credential inserts

rubric.name: Earned Authority — Claims Grounded, Not Asserted
rubric.description: Every novelty claim, priority claim, or capability assertion is substantiated with specific technical, methodological, or empirical detail. A skeptical reviewer should finish each claim thinking 'I see why' rather than being asked to take it on faith. Descriptions of existing tools or data must not accidentally make the scientific challenge seem trivial or already solved.

dimensions.label: Novelty or 'first' claims are immediately followed by the specific detail that makes them true
Technical capabilities are described in concrete terms (mechanisms, specifications, design features) rather than generic competence language
Key methodological risks or challenges are named and reframed as the rationale for the work rather than hidden or hedged

Descriptions of available tools, data, or existing work do not accidentally collapse the perceived difficulty of the scientific challenge — the gap should feel harder to close as context accumulates, not easier
Superlatives and strong claims are absent unless backed by evidence in the same passage

writing_type: NIH grant proposal sections (significance, specific aims, innovation)
user_goals_summary: The user writes NIH proposals for mixed-discipline review panels and prioritizes narrative momentum, urgency, and cross-disciplinary accessibility above all else. They want every section to feel like a story about why this work matters right now — not a literature review or methods summary — with claims that are grounded in specific detail rather than asserted, and closing beats that land with earned emotional and scientific weight.
coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is narrative momentum — they will always prioritize story over completeness, and any passage that feels like a 'literature review' or 'methods section' dropped into the significance will be flagged immediately. They are exceptionally sensitive to how paragraphs end: closings must be declarative, must carry emotional weight proportional to the paragraph's strongest moment, and must assert forward motion rather than pose open questions. When given options, they consistently choose urgency-through-temporal-cost framing ('every year this goes unanswered') over paradox or team-positioning framings. They respond well to being pushed back on when an instinct (e.g., adding patient-facing language in a technical paragraph) would undermine structural coherence — but the pushback must name the trade-off precisely.

rubric.name: Narrative Arc & 'Why Now' Urgency
rubric.description: The significance section tells a story — not a literature review. Each sentence should make the gap feel more urgent than the last, until the proposed work feels like the only logical next step. The reader should feel the cost of inaction, not just understand that a gap exists.

dimensions.label: The scientific gap is framed as a tension or paradox the reader feels, not just a missing item in the literature
Each sentence in the significance section adds pressure — the gap feels more urgent as the section progresses, not just restated
The section reads as a narrative with logical progression, not as a sequence of disconnected claims or statistics
The reader feels the cost of not doing this work — urgency is conveyed through stakes and temporal cost, not just importance claims

By the end of the significance section, the proposed work feels like the necessary next step — arrived at through the convergence of specific assets the reader has watched accumulate, not asserted through exclusivity claims. Avoid 'no other team / no other cohort' constructions — inevitability should be a conclusion the reader reaches, not a challenge the writer issues.

Paragraph and section endings deliver earned resolution — they assert rather than announce, and their emotional register matches the strongest moments in the passage. Assertion is achieved through specific convergence of named assets, not through exclusivity claims or rhetorical challenges that invite a skeptical reviewer to push back.

The sense that the proposed work is the right next step is built by naming the specific assets, conditions, or capabilities that have converged — not by asserting the absence of alternatives. The reader should feel they have followed a logical accumulation to an obvious conclusion.

rubric.name: Standalone-Yet-Connected Aim Architecture
rubric.description: Each aim produces something independently valuable — a deliverable, a resolved question, or a contribution the field gains regardless of other aims. At the same time, completing all aims together produces something greater than the sum of parts. Reviewers penalize both disconnected aims and fragile dependency chains — this balance is the target.

dimensions.label: The aim has at least one concrete deliverable or scientific contribution that exists even if subsequent aims fail
Language avoids framing the aim as 'establishing the foundation' or being valuable only because it enables later work
When all aims are read together, a reviewer can see that their combination produces a scientific contribution beyond what any single aim delivers alone
The aim's closing naturally raises the next scientific question as a consequence of its findings, not as a structural dependency
The aim reads as a complete scientific argument with its own hypothesis, logic, and resolution — not a methods summary
Each aim is focused enough to be achievable — it does not try to answer multiple major questions or serve as a proposal within a proposal

rubric.name: Innovation: Names What's New AND What It Replaces
rubric.description: The innovation section must identify the current standard approach and explain specifically why it falls short before claiming what is new. Innovation is relative — reviewers cannot appreciate what's novel unless they understand what it replaces. The section should make the old approach feel inadequate and the new approach feel like the only credible path forward.

dimensions.label: The existing approach or tool being replaced or improved upon is explicitly named and described
The shortcoming of the current approach is stated in concrete, specific terms — not as a vague 'gap' but as a named failure mode or limitation
The proposed approach is described in direct contrast to the current one — the reader can see exactly what changes and why it matters
The innovation claim is substantiated with specific technical or conceptual detail — not asserted with adjectives like 'novel' or 'transformative' alone
The innovation section makes clear whether the proposed approach replaces, extends, or reframes the existing one — the relationship is explicit

rubric.name: Cross-Disciplinary Accessibility
rubric.description: Technical arguments include plain-language moments that let non-specialist reviewers feel the significance without the prose being dumbed down. Technical detail is subordinated to narrative flow — introduced as evidence supporting the argument, never as standalone inventories that break the story's momentum.

dimensions.label: At least one sentence per major paragraph translates the technical point into language a non-specialist reviewer can immediately grasp
Technical precision is maintained — accessibility is achieved through additional clarity, not by removing specificity
Key arguments are framed to resonate across the likely reviewer disciplines, not just the primary field
Specialized terms are introduced in context so their meaning is inferrable even to adjacent-field reviewers
Technical specifics are introduced as evidence within the narrative argument, not as standalone inventories that break the story's momentum

rubric.name: Strategic Framing of Preliminary Work & Personnel
rubric.description: Pilot data is positioned as a feasibility gate — proof that the team can do this work and that the next question is now answerable. It must not read as a highlight reel of accomplishments. The message is 'we can do this,' not 'we already did this.' Personnel descriptions belong in the scientific argument, not a credential list.

dimensions.label: Preliminary data is framed as proving feasibility and revealing what remains to be answered, not as previewing results
The pilot framing makes clear what the funded work will do that the pilot cannot — the 'already done' problem is actively avoided
Claims that tools, methods, or infrastructure are 'ready' are accompanied by the specific gap that still requires funded investigation
Key personnel are introduced through the scientific or methodological condition they satisfy — why this team makes this question tractable
Personnel descriptions are woven into the scientific argument rather than appearing as credential inserts
Every claim about resources, personnel allocation, or timeline is explicitly connected to a specific scientific goal — reviewers can trace each logistical decision back to the science it enables

rubric.name: Earned Authority — Claims Grounded, Not Asserted
rubric.description: Every novelty claim, priority claim, or capability assertion is substantiated with specific technical, methodological, or empirical detail. A skeptical reviewer should finish each claim thinking 'I see why' rather than being asked to take it on faith. Risks must be named — overpromising without acknowledging uncertainty is a dealbreaker.

dimensions.label: Novelty or 'first' claims are immediately followed by the specific detail that makes them true
Technical capabilities are described in concrete terms rather than generic competence language
Key methodological risks or challenges are named explicitly and reframed as the rationale for the work — not hidden, hedged, or buried
Descriptions of available tools, data, or existing work do not accidentally collapse the perceived difficulty of the scientific challenge
Superlatives and strong claims are absent unless backed by evidence in the same passage

The writing uses active voice that names who is doing the work — passive constructions that obscure agency are avoided throughout. Active voice is most critical in procedural and capability claims — in closing or climactic sentences, the priority is grounded specificity over grammatical agency alone.
